content.assessment: Investing in comprehensive schools without touching track structure is politically the least contentious option since it avoids provoking the church and middle-class veto constituencies altogether, and does not engage the admissions-reform framing that has already proven politically explosive. Its feasibility rests on sustained public legitimacy as a credible equity strategy rather than on any coalition negotiation over track structure.
opportunities: Could be positioned as a consensus-building first step, potentially building future political capital and public legitimacy toward eventual structural reform
strengths: Bypasses the veto constituencies protecting track structure entirely, since it neither reforms admissions nor delays selection age [F-live-political_feasibility-05, F-live-political_feasibility-07]
Does not trigger the politically volatile 'structural transformation' framing that has affected other proposals [F-live-political_feasibility-04]
threats: Because it avoids addressing the selection mechanisms directly, if visible inequality indicators persist despite investment, it could undermine public legitimacy of a 'reform without reform' narrative over time [F-live-political_feasibility-03]
weaknesses: Divided teacher-union opinion on whether track reform or broader anti-segregation investment is the correct priority could dilute unified professional-coalition backing for this compensatory approach [F-live-political_feasibility-06]
